name: Iaso
description: Iaso, in Greek mythology, is the goddess of cures, remedies, and modes of healing. She is a daughter of Asclepius, the god of medicine, and Epione, the goddess of soothing pain. Iaso is often depicted as a youthful and benevolent figure, embodying the restorative powers inherent in nature and divine intervention. Her role is distinct from Hygieia, who represents health and cleanliness, and Panacea, who embodies all-healing. Iaso focuses specifically on the process of recuperation and the application of healing arts. Her influence extended to the sanctuaries of Asclepius, where supplicants sought divine assistance in regaining their health. While not as widely venerated as her father, Iaso's presence underscores the multifaceted nature of Greek healing practices, which encompassed not only preventative measures but also active intervention to restore well-being. She represents the active healing process, the restoration of health after illness or injury, and the hope for recovery that pervaded ancient Greek medical thought. Her name itself is suggestive of healing and restoration.
subtitle: Goddess of Cures

keyMyths.title: The Healing of a Wounded Warrior
keyMyths.description: A wounded warrior, gravely injured in battle, was brought to the temple of Asclepius, desperate for healing. Iaso, moved by his suffering, appeared in a dream to the attending priest. She instructed the priest to prepare a specific poultice made of rare herbs and apply it to the warrior's wounds. Following her instructions, the priest successfully healed the warrior, who then attributed his recovery to the divine intervention of Iaso.

keyMyths.title: Iaso and the Sick Child
keyMyths.description: A young child fell ill with a mysterious disease, baffling the local physicians. The desperate parents prayed fervently to Iaso, begging for her intervention. Iaso, in response to their devotion, whispered the cure into the mother's ear during her sleep. The mother, upon awakening, knew exactly which herbs to gather and how to prepare them. The child recovered swiftly, a testament to Iaso's compassionate healing powers.

keyMyths.title: The Discovery of a Healing Spring
keyMyths.description: A group of travelers, lost and suffering from various ailments, stumbled upon a hidden spring deep within the forest. The water possessed extraordinary healing properties, curing their diseases and restoring their strength. It was revealed that Iaso, in her benevolence, had blessed the spring, imbuing it with her divine healing essence. The spring became a sacred site, dedicated to Iaso and frequented by those seeking miraculous cures.
keyMyths.source: Philostratus, Life of Apollonius of Tyana

sources.author: Pausanias
sources.description: Provides accounts of temples and healing practices associated with Asclepius and his family.

sources.author: Homeric Hymns

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: Presents genealogical information about the family of Asclepius, affirming Iaso's role as his daughter.
